questions.questionText: For cancer pain, morphine is the gold standard strong opioid. Its most common side effect that does NOT develop tolerance is:
questions.optionB: Constipation (opioid-induced constipation persists throughout treatment — always co-prescribe laxatives with long-term opioids)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Constipation is the only opioid side effect that does NOT develop tolerance. Why correct: Opioids reduce GI motility via mu-receptors in myenteric plexus → constipation persists throughout treatment (no tolerance develops). Always prescribe stimulant laxative (bisacodyl/senna) prophylactically. Why A wrong: Sedation — tolerance develops within days. Why C wrong: Respiratory depression — tolerance develops rapidly. Why D wrong: Nausea — tolerance develops within 5-7 days (anti-emetics only needed initially). Clinical relevance: WHO ladder Step 3 = strong opioids (morphine oral first-line). Naloxone: reverses respiratory depression (half-life shorter than morphine → may need re-dosing). Exam trap: Tolerance develops to ALL opioid effects EXCEPT constipation and miosis. This is a classic MCQ fact. Memory aid: 'Constipation = Constant (no tolerance ever).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Opioid Pharmacology.

questions.questionText: Neuropathic pain is best treated with:
questions.optionB: Adjuvant analgesics: gabapentin/pregabalin (calcium channel α2δ ligands) or TCAs (amitriptyline) or SNRIs (duloxetine) as first-line
questions.explanation: Core concept: Neuropathic pain requires adjuvant analgesics, not standard analgesics. Why correct: Neuropathic pain (burning, shooting, tingling — nerve damage) responds poorly to conventional analgesics. First-line: gabapentin/pregabalin (binds α2δ subunit of calcium channels), TCAs (amitriptyline — antidepressant + analgesic), SNRIs (duloxetine). Why A wrong: NSAIDs target inflammatory pain (prostaglandin pathway) — ineffective for neuropathic. Why C wrong: Paracetamol — mild analgesic, ineffective for neuropathic. Why D wrong: Aspirin — anti-inflammatory, not neuropathic. Clinical relevance: Examples: diabetic neuropathy, post-herpetic neuralgia, trigeminal neuralgia (carbamazepine first-line for this specific type), phantom limb pain. Exam trap: Trigeminal neuralgia = carbamazepine (NOT gabapentin first-line). All other neuropathic = gabapentin/pregabalin/TCA. Memory aid: 'Neuropathic = Nerve pain = Need Gabapentin/Pregabalin (not NSAIDs).' Reference: Aitkenhead – Pain Management.
questions.conceptTag: Neuropathic pain gabapentin pregabalin TCA adjuvant
